item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I…interpreted this as implying that, if you fail to prevent a loved one's suicide, you deserve to be shot in the head. Took coming to the comments to get the apparent actual meaning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There isn't a plot twist, this is just a good item. good ideas have a wholeness to themselves akin to the structure of a story. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It took me a couple reads to wrap my head around it, but I quite like it. Generally speaking, I much prefer longer, more detailed SCPs; they feel more 'thorough' and 'scientific' to me. However, I enjoy this specifically because it is trying to be as concise as possible, as I also love the format screws (and, in my head, having a long detailed entry is a part of the 'correct format' for an SCP). The subversion of expectation with the collapsible at the end is really what sells the whole thing and ties it together.

I will also echo the sentiment that I think this would be better served in multiple ways by shortening the time between the first and second attempts. I'd much prefer it if the gunshot-attempt and the wrist-slitting-attempt happened on the same day. Still, +1 for overall quality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just wanted to say that, since I've read it, this has been one of my favorite SCPs. It says everything it needs to, and nothing more, and I love that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my goodness, I only got it from reading your comment. My idea was that they couldn't figure out what the gun did so they just used it on D-Class until they realized that they weren't getting anything done. It only just now hit me. Damn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how the last sentence that makes everything mysterious, like a plot twist. I understand it a bit but thanks for the spoiler Author. (I'm not being sarcastic)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really mixed feelings on that one. While I got what's it about I get how it's a bit cryptic. The short lenght helps to make it a real gut punch, but at he same time feels a bit lazy. As a skip serving cheap emotions based on suicide and loss of family it doesn't really do it. On the other hand I think it's great at almost breaking the fourth wall to ask the reader: "Why are you expecting more? Why would you think anyone would experiment with it and not just lock it up? Why would the Foundation sacrifice lives to confirm what we all know already? For your amusement?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it is too unclear about the properties. The 'timeline' is confusing. I suggest changing it from "Three days before the discovery of his body, his college roommate, his mother…" to "On the date of his death, his college roommate, his mother…" or something similar. Spell out that the suicide and the loved ones being shot happened at the same time, in other words. Maybe add something about 3 bullets missing from the magazine if you can bear to ignore the original goal of "lowest word count"
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took me a few minutes to get it. "I get that it kills the loved ones of the person it's shot at, but there wasn't anyone shoo- ohhhhhhh. Oh nooooo."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is incredibly powerful and useful. If the Foundation has an enemy who is completely in hiding, just take someone who loves that person and shoot them.

…And keep shooting til it hits who you were aiming for.

Yeah the Foundation definitely needs to lock this up. It'd be a pretty good tool to kill an O-5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't get it at first, to be honest with you.

Then I did. +1

item.title: Re: Too Short
item.content:encoded: Yeah okay, and? It's a gun that targets the victim's closest people, okay. It's a sad story about how suicide hurts the people you love, okay. But it's still 129 words. It's a nice little story, but it doesn't work at all in term of SCP article. At least for me, and I honestly don't really get how it got so popular, but heh, what do I know…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not anti-gun, it's just saying that suicide hurts the people you're close to.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: The message came across clearly to me. Obviously, being as short as it is it'll come across vague to some people, but I think the author did a great job. Definitely upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article wasn't clear enough on what the object does for my tastes. I realize that this is subjective and some people prefer some things unsaid in the article so they can infer for themselves. Personally I tend to like things to be spelled out a little more. On my first reading I thought that the story was a murder-suicide and I still feel like that's a plausible interpretation based on the information given in the article.

Once I read the comments, I really liked the story that this article tells. This guy was feeling so much at rock-bottom that he decided to end it, and instead he ends up shooting his friends and family and feeling even worse. The universe is really kicking a guy while he's down, there. But I didn't like that I had to read the comments to figure that out. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
harming his loved ones literally, it's also a pretty concise metaphor for the damage you do to those who love you when you take your own life.

…And this changed my neutral vote to a plus one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They don't know it though. The knowledge about this thing's anomalous properties comes from the implications, and not from the in-universe exploration. And that's my problem with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: maybe i just don't get the appeal to this? this is short and it feels like a series I skip where it's a magic thing for the sake of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How CAN they study it, though? They can't test it with D-Class, because D-Class tend to have family and friends outside of jail. There's pretty much no way they can test it without endangering innocents.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: The spoilers are by no means the only way to come to this conclusion. The spoilers are only here for those who didn't get it — it's not uncommon practice on the site.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Who said it's impossible? You have all the clues for you there in the article.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
It's a gun that kills loved ones of the target shot.

If it's impossible to understand the object by reading the article and related documents, without any "author spoilers" — I doubt that the idea is one of the best.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have conflicting feelings about this. I get that it kills your loved ones instead of you, and this effect is a metaphor of how you're hurting people who love you by committing suicide.

But I'll be honest, the execution doesn't do much for me. The overall simplicity isn't redeemed by the concept, so the skip ends up looking bare-bones.

I also see the final collapsible as an attempt to add more weight to this concept, but the way it's done seems a little strange. I don't have anything against more "white" Foundation, but in this case they just found the item and gave it an SCP designation without even studying it properly. And it happened just because the out-of-universe message had to be conveyed. This structure with "unknown anomalous properties" seems like a crutch made for us to get the point of this skip ourselves, which kinda ruins it for me.

Overall, it's a downvote from me. But I'll say that the message behind this is very clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No need to use strikeout, I agree with you

Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Admittedly, I think some of the more elaborate SCP's that have been posted to Series Five deserve more recognition than this but…

This is great. The realization hits like a ton of bricks.

item.title: Re: Too Short
item.content:encoded: Think about why the tests were canceled and how they found the anomaly in the first place.

item.title: Too Short
item.content:encoded: I mean, its like five sentences, and what is the point of the testing logs it it just says cancelled, I don't really see why people like this so much. If I missed something please elaborate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Woah.

+1

In under a week!? Jesus Christ you guys! That’s insane! Thank you all for reading it (not that it took up much of your time, heheh), special thanks to whoever made the typo “loss pistol” that spawned this article, special thanks to my critiquers whom I’ve forgotten to acknowledge (I believe they were DrAnnoyingDog and Uncle Nicolini and some others). This is my fastest +100 ever! Exciting!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, no, Captain Kirby had a similar issue which he suggested fixing by getting rid of the collapsible, but I tried a version of the article like that for a hot second and then people complained. I’m certain you can find it somewhere in the revisions, but I’m not sure which one. Anyways, I get where you’re coming from, but I’m gonna stick with this execution. Glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I adore this as a piece of fiction, in very few words it sends a gut-wrenching message. It is, for me at least, genuinely heartbreaking.

I'll admit though that as documantation, it's hard for me to reconcile that it's stated that the properties are unknown and that they mean to test it, but then decide against it for ethical concerns. Of course, it's simple enough to infer what it may do, but to state that there are concerns without a direct indication as to why feels sloppy.

I didn't notice that anyone alse had the same issue, but I think the inclusion of that progression in the Foundation's involvement (however miniscule it actually is) is really distracting.

This is an odd case of pulling off something extraordinarily well that has really fallen by the wayside in the past several years: leading the Description with an object's recovery rather than its anomalous properties. Granted, ha, it's basically all it is, but my point is that that's truly the meat of the article; the brief zigzag of the Foundation's interest in testing it removes some of the impact.

I do have a suggestion that seems to me like it would maintainthe integrity of the narrative, while also excising what feels like an excessive story beat:

+ show block
– hide block

SCP-4465 is a Glock 17 pistol.

SCP-4465 was found in an apartment along with the body of Arnold Dubar, who committed suicide through exsanguination from the wrists. Three days before the discovery of his body, his college roommate, his mother, and his family dog had all suffered identical gunshot wounds, from the roof of the mouth to the back of the head, within ten minutes of each other. Bullets matched those in SCP-4465's magazine. Arnold Dubar was 3,000 kilometers away from the nearest victim.

There are no plans to determine the exact properties of SCP-4465.

It's a touch lackluster, the first line feels a little too thin all on its lonesome, and I'm not sure I'm wholly satisfied with my edit of the last line, but the idea was to kill the extra beat, so eh.

Think I may be the only one that has this issue, so ymmv.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I likened these articles to one another because I read them in nearly the same way. I read them and got the gist, realized that there must be more to the article than what I was gleaning from it, and then I reread them and got the true implications.

I guess from the author's perspective, salsa hammer might be beating the reader over the head (heh) with its implications, but I (amongst many others) didn't read it that way at first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think Khaim is implying that the spoilered section isn't subtle at all. I'd agree, but then I'm a little biased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How is Salsa Hammer not subtle? That article is loaded with fridge logic that it seems you missed on your first read. Yeah sure, it's just a straightforward article about a hammer that turns stuff into salsa, until you realize:

+ Spoiler for Salsa Hammer
- Spoiler for Salsa Hammer

They beat some guy in the head with the hammer, let him squirm around headless, and then ate his fucking salsa brains, killing him 30 minutes after they were eaten.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … How is Salsa Hammer subtle?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure how I slipped past this one. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Before this, the shortest mainlist SCP on the site was SCP-2165, standing at 148 words.

This is 129 words.

You absolute madman. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The obvious being that the gun shoots your loved ones instead of you or anyone directed at.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yayyy someone spelled out the metaphor <3

Glad to have changed your vote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I initially hated this scip because I didn't really get it until I thought hard about the author post. The fridge logic only hit me three articles later.

+ show block
– hide block

I realized that he was trying to off himself with the glock first, but although it fired it didn't hurt him. He eventually gave up and offed himself by cutting his wrists. Not only is it a nice twist that his attempted suicide-by-magic-glock was harming his loved ones literally, it's also a pretty concise metaphor for the damage you do to those who love you when you take your own life.

In short, my -1 is now a +1.

item.title: Re: A Couple of Questions
item.content:encoded:
Ooooh.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sometimes, a story is just a story. An afterglow reflected by cold ice mirrors.

item.title: Re: A Couple of Questions
item.content:encoded: You got it, and I’ll leave the last bit up to interpretation ;)

item.title: A Couple of Questions
item.content:encoded:
Spoiler/s? 
Close it

Did the dude tried to kill himself using the gun, not knowing it's anomalous property and the reason why three of his loved ones was killed is because he tried to shoot the gun three times when it didn't blew his brains out on the first three tries?

Did he know he caused their deaths? Or did he just know that they died?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An interesting perspective on the Foundation actually not testing something because of ethics. I'm sure the committee is proud of themselves. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool concept, but it's a bit long winded. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no answer, because nobody wanted to ask the question…
I don’t usually celebrate +50, but this is not just +50… it’s +50 in under 12 hours!! That’s insane!! I’m so happy for the reception this has gotten, it is truly wonderful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I support this. The collapsible is a huge thing for me. I would've dislike this skip if I wasn't baited. Before reading the article, the first thing I did was click the collapsible because I suspected that it must be a long one. But it isn't. I felt interested immediately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because I started looking for a deeper message behind it when I didn't like it at first

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. The site needs more short articles like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aside from the obvious, this is too short to be considered as a message at all.

I'm curious as to what you mean from "the obvious". I did write this with a message in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My thoughts were initially that this was some Anti-Gun message but reading the spoiler has changed my view on it.

Why were your first thoughts focused on this being an Anti-Gun message? Aside from the obvious, this is too short to be considered as a message at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really clever, but there's not really a lot expanded enough to my liking, I'm afraid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I just too stupid to get whatever is happening here?

Yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like the "there is no answer to why this happened" vibe to this article. Which is sorta spooky and allows you to make assumptions on what the anomalous properties are. So for that +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This almost leaves too many questions unanswered, but I really admire the subtlety and economy here, as well as the idea that there's something capable of ethically stumping the Foundation. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn. There seem to be factions. I have some people in IRC saying to keep the collapsible, and some saying not to. Grrrr. I hate stuff like this.

EDIT: Keeping the collapsible and that's final. No more edits.
